narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite coming from a lower-middle income family, and we appreciate the full-time commitment you're ready to make to your studies. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 73.8% chance that you would not pass, which is why your application was rejected—schools rely on this to ensure strong outcomes for their students. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings in school, particularly your position in the lowest decile for Year 3 grades, which had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward expecting a bar exam failure. Your LSAT score of 26 also played a significant role, further tipping the scales in that direction compared to what the model typically sees in successful students. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 stood out as a helpful factor, pulling the prediction toward success since it's close to the average and showed some resilience in your college performance. Your Year 1 decile ranking in the lowest group added another nudge toward the failure prediction, though less strongly than Year 3. Meanwhile, your family income background and choice to study full-time—aligning with the vast majority of students who succeed—provided smaller boosts toward passing. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having below-average LSAT and early academic deciles that outweighed your solid GPA and personal circumstances. While this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black women like you face in higher education, it doesn't define your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You have the drive—keep pushing, and many paths to law remain open. We're rooting for you.
